content:
# Exodus Chapter 24 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~46r–46v (PDF pp. 90–91); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 66–67 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_024_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_024_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_024_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_024_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

## The Summons to Covenant Ratification (vv. 1–2)

**1** And to Moses [**ve'el-Mosheh**] He said [**amar**], "Come up [**aleh**] to [**el**] YHWH [**YHWH**], you [**attah**] and Aaron [**ve'Aharon**], Nadab [**Nadav**] and Abihu [**va'Avihu**], and seventy [**veshiv'im**] of the elders [**miziqney**] of Israel [**Yisra'el**], and you shall bow down [**vehishtachavitem**] from afar [**merachoq**].

**2** And Moses alone [**veniggash Mosheh levaddo**] shall draw near [**veniggash**] to [**el**] YHWH [**YHWH**], but they [**vehem**] shall not draw near [**lo yiggashu**], and the people [**veha'am**] shall not come up [**lo ya'alu**] with him [**immo**]."

---

## The People's Acceptance and the Covenant Ceremony (vv. 3–8)

**3** And Moses came [**vayyavo Mosheh**] and told [**vayesapper**] the people [**la'am**] all [**et kol**] the words [**divrey**] of YHWH [**YHWH**] and all [**ve'et kol**] the judgments [**hammishpatim**], and all [**kol**] the people [**ha'am**] answered [**vayya'an**] with one voice [**qol echad**] and said [**vayyomeru**], "All [**kol**] the words [**haddevarim**] which [**asher**] YHWH [**YHWH**] has spoken [**dibber**] we will do [**na'aseh**]."

**4** And Moses wrote [**vayyikhtov Mosheh**] all [**et-kol**] the words [**divrey**] of YHWH [**YHWH**], and he rose early [**vayyashkem**] in the morning [**babboqer**] and built [**vayyiven**] an altar [**mizbe'ach**] at the foot of [**tachat**] the mountain [**hahar**], and twelve [**shtem esreh**] pillars [**mattsevah**] for the twelve [**lishnem asar**] tribes [**shivtey**] of Israel [**Yisra'el**].

**5** And he sent [**vayyishlach**] the young men [**et-na'arey**] of the Sons of Israel [**beney Yisra'el**], and they offered [**vayya'alu**] burnt offerings [**olot**] and sacrificed [**vayyizbechu**] peace offerings [**zevachim shelamim**] to YHWH [**laYHWH**] — bulls [**parim**].

**6** And Moses took [**vayyiqqach Mosheh**] half [**chatsi**] of the blood [**haddam**] and put it [**vayyasem**] in basins [**ba'agganot**], and half [**vachatsi**] of the blood [**haddam**] he threw [**zaraq**] upon [**al**] the altar [**hammizbe'ach**].

**7** And he took [**vayyiqqach**] the Book [**sefer**] of the Covenant [**habberit**] and read [**vayyiqra**] in the ears [**be'ozney**] of the people [**ha'am**], and they said [**vayyomeru**], "All [**kol**] that [**asher**] YHWH [**YHWH**] has spoken [**dibber**] we will do [**na'aseh**] and we will hear [**venishma**]."

**8** And Moses took [**vayyiqqach Mosheh**] the blood [**et-haddam**] and threw it [**vayyizroq**] upon [**al**] the people [**ha'am**] and said [**vayyomer**], "Behold [**hinneh**] the blood [**dam**] of the covenant [**habberit**] which [**asher**] YHWH [**YHWH**] has cut [**karat**] with you [**immakhem**] concerning [**al**] all [**kol**] these [**ha'eleh**] words [**haddevarim**]."

---

## The Vision of the God of Israel (vv. 9–11)

**9** And Moses went up [**vayyaal Mosheh**], and Aaron [**ve'Aharon**], Nadab [**Nadav**] and Abihu [**va'Avihu**], and seventy [**veshiv'im**] of the elders [**miziqney**] of Israel [**Yisra'el**].

**10** And they saw [**vayyir'u**] the God [**Elohey**] of Israel [**Yisra'el**], and under [**vetachat**] His feet [**raglav**] was something like a work [**kema'aseh**] of sapphire [**livnat**] pavement [**hassappir**], and like the very [**uke'etsem**] heavens [**hashamayim**] for clearness [**latohar**].

**11** And against [**ve'el**] the nobles [**atsiley**] of the Sons of Israel [**beney Yisra'el**] He did not stretch out [**lo shalach**] His hand [**yado**], and they beheld [**vayyechezu**] God [**et-ha'Elohim**], and they ate [**vayyokhelu**] and they drank [**vayyishtu**].

---

## Moses Ascends the Mountain (vv. 12–14)

**12** And YHWH [**YHWH**] said [**vayyomer**] to [**el**] Moses [**Mosheh**], "Come up [**aleh**] to Me [**elay**] to the mountain [**haharah**] and be [**vehyeh**] there [**sham**], and I will give [**ve'ettenah**] to you [**lekha**] the tablets [**et-luchot**] of stone [**ha'even**] and the law [**vehatorah**] and the commandment [**vehamitsvah**] which [**asher**] I have written [**katavti**] to instruct them [**lehorotam**]."

**13** And Moses rose up [**vayyaqom Mosheh**] and Joshua [**viYehoshua**] his attendant [**mesharto**], and Moses went up [**vayyaal Mosheh**] to [**el**] the mountain [**har**] of God [**ha'Elohim**].

**14** And to the elders [**ve'el-hazzeqenim**] he said [**amar**], "Wait [**shevu**] for us [**lanu**] here [**vazeh**] until [**ad**] we return [**asher-nashuv**] to you [**aleykhem**], and behold [**vehinneh**] Aaron [**Aharon**] and Hur [**veChur**] are with you [**immakhem**]. Whoever [**mi**] has [**ba'al**] a matter [**devarim**], let him draw near [**yiggash**] to them [**alehem**]."

---

## The Glory of YHWH on the Mountain (vv. 15–18)

**15** And Moses went up [**vayyaal Mosheh**] to [**el**] the mountain [**hahar**], and the cloud [**he'anan**] covered [**vayekhas**] the mountain [**et-hahar**].

**16** And the glory [**kevod**] of YHWH [**YHWH**] settled [**vayyishkon**] upon [**al**] Mount [**har**] Sinai [**Sinay**], and the cloud [**he'anan**] covered it [**vayekhassehu**] six days [**sheshet yamim**], and He called [**vayyiqra**] to [**el**] Moses [**Mosheh**] on the seventh day [**bayyom hashevi'i**] from the midst of [**mittokh**] the cloud [**he'anan**].

**17** And the appearance [**umar'eh**] of the glory [**kevod**] of YHWH [**YHWH**] was like a consuming [**okhelet**] fire [**esh**] on the top of [**berosh**] the mountain [**hahar**] before the eyes of [**le'eyney**] the Sons of Israel [**beney Yisra'el**].

**18** And Moses entered [**vayyavo Mosheh**] into the midst of [**betokh**] the cloud [**he'anan**] and went up [**vayyaal**] to [**el**] the mountain [**hahar**], and Moses was [**vayhi Mosheh**] on the mountain [**bahar**] forty [**arba'im**] days [**yom**] and forty [**ve'arba'im**] nights [**laylah**].

---

## Translator's Notes

### Key Terms

**vehishtachavitem merachoq** (v. 1) -- "and you shall bow down from afar." The verb hishtachavah (Hishtaphel of sh-ch-h, "to bow down, prostrate oneself") is the standard term for worship-prostration throughout the Hebrew Bible. The command to bow "from afar" (merachoq) establishes a graduated hierarchy of access to the divine presence: Moses alone may draw near; Aaron, Nadab, Abihu, and the seventy elders may ascend but must worship at a distance; the people may not ascend at all (v. 2). This threefold gradation -- Moses / the priestly-elder company / the people -- anticipates the architecture of the Tabernacle with its Most Holy Place (accessible to the high priest alone), the Holy Place (accessible to priests), and the outer court (accessible to the laity). The spatial theology of Sinai becomes the architectural theology of the Mishkan. The LXX renders merachoq as makrothen ("from afar"), preserving the spatial distinction precisely.

**na'asey venishma** (v. 7) -- "we will do and we will hear." This is one of the most celebrated phrases in the entire Torah, and its word order is theologically momentous. The people do not say "we will hear and then do" (nishma vena'aseh) but "we will do and we will hear" (na'aseh venishma) -- placing obedient action before comprehensive understanding. The Talmud (Shabbat 88a) records that when Israel said na'aseh venishma, a heavenly voice exclaimed, "Who revealed to My children this secret that the ministering angels use?" -- for the angels too perform God's word before they fully understand it (cf. Ps 103:20, "who do His word, to hear the voice of His word"). The phrase expresses a radical epistemology of obedience: true understanding of God's will comes through doing it, not prior to doing it. In v. 3, the people had said only na'aseh ("we will do"); now in v. 7, after hearing the Book of the Covenant read aloud, they add venishma ("and we will hear/obey") -- an intensification of commitment. The LXX renders this as poiēsomen kai akousometha ("we will do and we will hear/obey"), faithfully preserving the Hebrew word order that is so theologically significant.

**sefer habberit** (v. 7) -- "the Book of the Covenant." This is the only place in the Torah where this exact phrase appears, and it retroactively names the entire legal corpus that Moses has written down -- minimally the ordinances of 21:1-23:33, and possibly including the Decalogue of 20:1-17. The act of writing (v. 4, vayyikhtov) and public reading (v. 7, vayyiqra) constitutes the first recorded instance of textual covenant ratification in Scripture. The written document becomes the authoritative reference point for the covenant relationship -- a pattern that will recur at covenant renewals under Joshua (Josh 24:26), Josiah (2 Kgs 23:2), and Ezra (Neh 8:1-8). The LXX renders sefer habberit as to biblion tēs diathēkēs ("the book of the covenant/testament"), using diathēkē -- the term that will become the standard Greek word for "covenant" and eventually gives its name to the "New Testament" (kainē diathēkē).

**dam habberit** (v. 8) -- "the blood of the covenant." The blood ritual of vv. 6-8 is the central act of covenant ratification, and its structure is precise: half the blood is thrown on the altar (representing God's side of the covenant), and half is thrown on the people (representing Israel's side). The blood thus binds both parties -- God and people -- in a single covenantal bond. The phrase dam habberit appears again only in Zechariah 9:11 ("As for you also, because of the blood of your covenant, I will set your prisoners free") and is directly quoted by Jesus at the Last Supper: "This is My blood of the covenant" (to haima mou tēs diathēkēs, Matt 26:28; Mark 14:24), with the author of Hebrews drawing an extended comparison between the Sinai blood-covenant and the new covenant in Christ's blood (Heb 9:18-22). The phrase thus becomes one of the most consequential in all of biblical theology, bridging the Sinai covenant and the new covenant. The LXX renders this as to haima tēs diathēkēs ("the blood of the covenant/testament"), the exact phrase that reappears in the New Testament institution narratives.

**livnat hassappir** (v. 10) -- "a pavement of sapphire" or "a work of sapphire brick." The noun livnah is a hapax legomenon in the Hebrew Bible -- it occurs only here. Its meaning is debated: it may derive from the root l-v-n ("to be white, to make bricks") and thus mean "brick-work, pavement" (related to levenah, "brick"), or it may be related to lavan ("white") and suggest a "whiteness" or "brilliance" of sapphire. The sappir is not the modern sapphire but lapis lazuli, the deep blue stone prized throughout the ancient Near East. The vision is thus of a pavement of lapis lazuli beneath the feet of God -- a floor of celestial blue, "like the very heavens for clearness" (ke'etsem hashamayim latohar). The comparison to the heavens is not merely aesthetic but cosmological: what the elders see beneath God's feet is the heavenly firmament itself, as though they are looking upward through the dome of the sky from below, but now they see it from above, as the floor of God's throne room. Ezekiel's throne vision (Ezek 1:26) echoes this image: "Above the firmament... was something like a throne in appearance, like sapphire stone" (ke'even-sappir). The LXX renders livnat hassappir as plinthon sappheirou ("a brick/tile of sapphire"), taking livnah in the sense of "brick" (plinthos) -- a reading that may preserve an allusion to the bricks (levenim) that Israel was forced to make in Egypt (Exod 1:14; 5:7-8). If so, the theological symbolism is stunning: the bricks of slavery have become the pavement of divine glory; what was once the instrument of oppression is now the floor beneath God's throne.

**vayyechezu et-ha'Elohim** (v. 11) -- "and they beheld God." The verb chazah ("to see, behold, gaze upon") is distinct from the more common ra'ah ("to see") used in v. 10. While ra'ah is the ordinary verb of sight, chazah carries overtones of visionary, prophetic, or contemplative seeing -- it is the root of chazon ("vision") and chozeh ("seer"). The shift from ra'ah (v. 10, "they saw the God of Israel") to chazah (v. 11, "they beheld God") may signal an intensification: first they saw, then they gazed with sustained contemplation. The statement that they "beheld God and ate and drank" is astonishing in a tradition that insists "no man can see Me and live" (Exod 33:20). The eating and drinking may represent a covenant meal -- the shared feast that seals the treaty between the divine suzerain and His vassal people -- and the mention of eating and drinking serves to confirm that the elders survived the encounter: they were not destroyed but nourished. The LXX renders vayyechezu as ōphthēsan ("they were seen / they appeared") in some manuscripts, but the Vaticanus reading preserves the sense of active vision. The juxtaposition of "He did not stretch out His hand against the nobles" (v. 11a) with "they beheld God and ate and drank" (v. 11b) implies that the theophany was dangerous -- it could have destroyed them -- but God restrained His lethal holiness and permitted them to see and live.

**luchot ha'even vehatorah vehamitsvah** (v. 12) -- "the tablets of stone and the law and the commandment." The stone tablets (luchot) are the physical medium of divine writing -- God Himself has inscribed them (asher katavti, "which I have written"). The phrase vehatorah vehamitsvah ("and the law and the commandment") is in apposition to luchot ha'even, specifying the content of the tablets. Torah here does not mean the entire Pentateuch but "instruction, teaching" -- the specific divine instruction inscribed on stone. Mitsvah ("commandment") is singular, possibly a collective singular encompassing all the commandments, or possibly pointing to the Decalogue as a unified commandment. The purpose clause lehorotam ("to instruct them") uses the Hiphil infinitive of y-r-h, the same root as torah -- creating an etymological link: the torah is given lehorot ("to instruct"), and the act of instruction (horah) is the living application of the torah. The LXX renders luchot ha'even as tas plakas tas lithinas ("the stone tablets") and adds a clarifying expansion, specifying the content as the law and the commandments.

**mesharto** (v. 13) -- "his attendant, his minister." The noun mesharet (from the Piel of sh-r-t, "to serve, minister") describes Joshua's role as Moses' personal aide and liturgical servant. The term is distinct from eved ("slave, servant") -- the mesharet serves voluntarily and in a position of honor, not bondage. Joshua's presence on the mountain, mentioned almost parenthetically, is narratively significant: he is the only person besides Moses who ascends beyond the point where the elders stop. This privileged access to the mountain of God prepares Joshua for his future role as Moses' successor. The LXX renders mesharto as ho leitourgos ("the liturgical minister"), a term with cultic associations that later gives English the word "liturgy."

**Chur** (v. 14) -- Hur (Chur) appears here and in Exodus 17:10-12 (supporting Moses' hands during the battle with Amalek) as a co-leader alongside Aaron. His sudden appearance without introduction in 17:10 and his pairing with Aaron as judicial authority during Moses' absence suggest he was a figure of considerable standing. The Chronicler identifies him as the son of Caleb and Ephrath (1 Chr 2:19-20) and the grandfather of Bezalel, the chief craftsman of the Tabernacle (Exod 31:2; 1 Chr 2:20). Jewish tradition (Josephus, Ant. 3.54; various midrashim) identifies Hur as the husband of Miriam, making him Moses' brother-in-law. His role here as co-regent with Aaron during Moses' absence is the last mention of Hur in the narrative of Exodus -- he disappears entirely from the golden calf episode (ch. 32), leading some rabbinic traditions to suggest that Hur was killed for opposing the idolatry, and that Aaron's capitulation was partly motivated by fear of the same fate.

**kevod YHWH** (v. 16) -- "the glory of YHWH." The noun kavod (from k-v-d, "to be heavy, weighty") denotes the visible, luminous manifestation of the divine presence -- the "weight" of God's reality made perceptible to the senses. In v. 16, the kavod "settles" (vayyishkon, from sh-k-n, "to dwell, tabernacle") on Mount Sinai -- the first use of this theologically critical verb in connection with the divine glory. The verb shakan will become the defining term for God's presence in the Tabernacle (the mishkan, from the same root) and in later Jewish theology gives rise to the concept of the Shekhinah -- the indwelling divine presence. The cloud (anan) that covers the mountain for six days before God speaks on the seventh day recapitulates the creation pattern: six days of preparatory covering, then the divine word on the seventh. The LXX renders kevod YHWH as hē doxa kuriou ("the glory of the Lord"), with doxa becoming the standard Greek theological term for divine glory -- the term that the New Testament applies to Christ (John 1:14, "we beheld His glory," etheasametha tēn doxan autou).

**esh okhelet** (v. 17) -- "a consuming fire." The appearance (mar'eh) of the glory of YHWH is described as "like a consuming fire" on the mountaintop. The phrase esh okhelet (literally, "fire eating/devouring") recurs in Deuteronomy 4:24 ("YHWH your God is a consuming fire, a jealous God") and 9:3, and is quoted in Hebrews 12:29 ("our God is a consuming fire"). The fire is seen "before the eyes of the Sons of Israel" -- the people at the base of the mountain can see the terrifying manifestation on the summit, into which Moses enters (v. 18). The visual contrast is dramatic: from below, the mountain appears to burn with devouring fire; Moses walks into this fire and survives for forty days. The LXX renders esh okhelet as pur phlegon ("blazing/burning fire"), using phlegō ("to burn, blaze") rather than a literal rendering of the "eating" metaphor in okhelet.

### Grammatical Notes

**ve'el-Mosheh amar** (v. 1) -- The chapter begins with a vav-conjunction and the prepositional phrase "and to Moses He said," with no explicit subject. The subject (YHWH) must be inferred from context -- He has been speaking since 20:22. The preposition el ("to, toward") rather than the more common le ("to") may suggest directionality: the command is issued toward Moses, who is perhaps still at a distance. The placement of this divine speech is chronologically debated: some commentators (Rashi, Ibn Ezra) argue that v. 1 was spoken before the Book of the Covenant (i.e., before 20:22), and that the narrative is not chronological. Others maintain the straightforward sequential reading. The LXX renders this with kai Mōusei eipen ("and to Moses He said"), preserving the Hebrew word order.

**veniggash Mosheh levaddo el-YHWH** (v. 2) -- "And Moses alone shall draw near to YHWH." The verb niggash (Niphal of n-g-sh) means "to draw near, approach." The adverb levaddo ("he alone, by himself") is emphatic: Moses' solitary access to the divine presence is underscored. This exclusive proximity is the defining mark of Mosaic prophecy: "mouth to mouth I speak with him" (Num 12:8). The spatial hierarchy -- Moses nearest, the priestly-elder company at a distance, the people at the base -- will be literalized in the Tabernacle's concentric zones of holiness.

**vayyizroq al-ha'am** (v. 8) -- "and he threw [it] upon the people." The verb zaraq means "to throw, toss, dash" -- it is a forceful, ritualized casting of blood, not a gentle sprinkling. The same verb is used for the blood thrown on the altar in v. 6. The symmetry is exact: zaraq on the altar (God's side), zaraq on the people (Israel's side). The act of blood-throwing creates a blood bond between God and people, mediated through the altar. This is not symbolic sprinkling but covenantal blood-application -- a solemn, violent act that binds both parties under penalty of death (the implicit logic being: may my blood be shed like this if I break this covenant).

**kema'aseh livnat hassappir** (v. 10) -- The syntax is complex. Ma'aseh ("work, workmanship, something made") is in construct with livnat, which is itself in construct with hassappir. The phrase could mean "like a work of sapphire pavement" (taking livnah as "pavement") or "like a construction of the whiteness of sapphire" (taking livnah from lavan, "white"). The ke- prefix indicates similitude: what they saw was not literally identified as sapphire brickwork but was "like" (ke-) such a thing. The visionary quality is preserved: the elders see something that transcends ordinary categories and can only be approximated through comparison.

**uke'etsem hashamayim latohar** (v. 10) -- "and like the very heavens for clearness." The noun etsem means "bone, substance, self, essence" -- used here in its abstract sense of "the very thing itself, the essence of." Hashamayim ("the heavens") is the sky. Latohar is the infinitive construct of t-h-r ("to be clean, pure") with the preposition le-, functioning as a specification of respect: "with respect to purity/clearness." The combined phrase means "like the very essence/substance of the heavens in its purity" -- i.e., like the sky on its most perfectly clear day, a blue of absolute luminous transparency. The LXX renders this as kai hōsper eidos stereōmatos tou ouranou tē kathariotēti ("and like the appearance of the firmament of heaven in purity"), introducing stereōma ("firmament") from Genesis 1:6-8.

**arba'im yom ve'arba'im laylah** (v. 18) -- "forty days and forty nights." The number forty is one of the most theologically significant numbers in Scripture. Moses' forty-day sojourn on Sinai will be repeated after the golden calf incident (Deut 9:18, 25; 10:10). The pattern recurs in Elijah's forty-day journey to Horeb (1 Kgs 19:8) and Jesus' forty-day fast in the wilderness (Matt 4:2). The number forty in Hebrew usage signifies a complete period of testing, preparation, or transformation: the flood lasted forty days (Gen 7:12), the spies explored Canaan for forty days (Num 13:25), and Israel wandered forty years in the wilderness (Num 14:33). Moses' forty days on the mountain are a period of intense divine-human communion during which God will give the instructions for the Tabernacle (chs. 25-31).

### Structural Notes

Exodus 24 is one of the most architecturally intricate chapters in the Pentateuch, serving as the hinge between two massive blocks of Sinaitic material: the Book of the Covenant (20:22-23:33, law) and the Tabernacle instructions (25:1-31:18, liturgy). The chapter narrates the formal ratification of the covenant whose terms have just been stipulated, and it simultaneously prepares for the next divine disclosure by moving Moses onto the mountain for forty days.

The chapter divides into four movements, each with distinct content and a distinct set of participants. The first movement (vv. 1-2) is a divine summons establishing the graduated hierarchy of access: Moses alone at the summit, the priestly-elder delegation at a distance, the people at the base. The second movement (vv. 3-8) is the covenant ratification proper, comprising the people's verbal assent (v. 3), Moses' writing and altar-building (v. 4), the sacrificial offerings (v. 5), the blood ritual (vv. 6, 8), and the public reading of the Book of the Covenant with the people's climactic response na'aseh venishma (v. 7). The third movement (vv. 9-11) is the theophanic ascent of the seventy elders, who behold the God of Israel and share a covenant meal in His presence -- a scene of breathtaking intimacy set against the backdrop of Sinai's lethal holiness. The fourth movement (vv. 12-18) narrates Moses' further ascent, alone, into the cloud of glory that crowns the mountain, where he will remain for forty days to receive the stone tablets and the Tabernacle plans.

The chapter's most striking structural feature is the apparent tension in its chronology. Verse 1 ("Come up to YHWH") seems to precede the covenant ceremony of vv. 3-8, yet the ascent does not occur until vv. 9-11. Many scholars have detected in this chapter the combination of two (or more) originally independent accounts of the covenant ratification. One account (vv. 3-8) centers on the blood ritual at the base of the mountain; the other (vv. 1-2, 9-11) centers on the theophanic vision at the summit. The final form of the text, however, presents these as sequential moments in a single ceremony: first the ratification by blood at the base (vv. 3-8), then the confirmatory vision and meal at the summit (vv. 9-11), and finally Moses' solitary ascent into the cloud (vv. 12-18). The canonical arrangement thus traces a progressive movement upward -- from the people at the base, through the elders on the mountain, to Moses alone in the cloud -- enacting spatially the theological principle of mediated access to God.

### Theological Notes

The blood ritual of vv. 6-8 constitutes the most detailed account of covenant ratification in the Hebrew Bible and is foundational for biblical theology of covenant, sacrifice, and atonement. The ritual has a precise bipartite structure: half the blood goes on the altar, half on the people. The altar represents YHWH's presence; the people represent themselves. The shared blood creates a bond of life between God and Israel -- they are now "blood relatives" in a covenantal sense. The intervening reading of the Book of the Covenant (v. 7) between the two blood-applications means that the covenant text is literally sandwiched between the two halves of the blood: first blood on the altar, then the words of the covenant, then blood on the people. Word and blood together constitute the covenant. Neither is sufficient alone: the blood without the word would be empty ritual; the word without the blood would be mere legislation. Together they create a covenant -- a bond that is simultaneously verbal (containing specific obligations) and vital (sealed in the substance of life).

The people's double response -- na'aseh in v. 3 and na'aseh venishma in v. 7 -- marks a deepening of covenantal commitment. Before the blood ritual, they pledge to do (na'aseh); after the blood ritual begins (with the altar-application of v. 6) and after the public reading (v. 7), they pledge both to do and to hear (na'aseh venishma). The addition of venishma suggests that covenant commitment is not a static moment but a dynamic process: initial obedience opens into deeper understanding. The rabbis were profoundly right to see in this word order a fundamental principle of the spiritual life: doing precedes understanding; practice is the school of theology.

The theophanic vision of vv. 9-11 is one of the most extraordinary passages in Scripture. Seventy-four people (Moses, Aaron, Nadab, Abihu, and seventy elders) see "the God of Israel" -- not merely an angel, not a symbolic representation, but Elohey Yisra'el. Yet the description focuses not on God's form but on what is beneath His feet: the sapphire pavement, clear as the sky. This reticence about the divine form is deliberate and theologically crucial: the elders see enough to know they are in the presence of God, but the text refuses to describe the divine figure itself. Deuteronomy 4:12 will later insist, "You heard the sound of words but saw no form (temunah) -- only a voice." The vision of Exodus 24 does not contradict this -- it describes the environs of the divine presence (the pavement, the sky-like clarity) but not the divine form. The theological principle is that God can be encountered -- genuinely, transformatively -- without being comprehended or depicted.

The statement that the elders "ate and drank" (v. 11) in the divine presence constitutes a covenant meal -- the ancient Near Eastern practice of sharing food to seal a treaty. The parallel with Genesis 31:46-54 (Jacob and Laban eat together to seal their covenant) and with 2 Samuel 3:20 (David and Abner feast to seal their pact) confirms the covenantal setting. But the Exodus 24 meal transcends all ancient parallels: here the meal partner is God Himself. The elders eat and drink in the presence of the God of Israel, on the mountain of God, having seen the God of Israel -- and they survive. This scene is the Old Testament's most intimate depiction of divine-human fellowship, and it stands as the archetypal covenant meal that later finds its fulfillment in the Passover, in the fellowship offerings at the Tabernacle, and ultimately in the eucharistic meal of the new covenant, where Jesus says, "This is My blood of the covenant" (Matt 26:28), deliberately echoing Moses' words in v. 8.

Moses' final ascent into the cloud (vv. 15-18) marks a transition from covenant ratification to covenant elaboration. The glory-cloud (kavod) that settles on Sinai is the visible sign of God's presence -- the same cloud that led Israel out of Egypt (13:21-22) and that will fill the completed Tabernacle (40:34-35). The six-day covering followed by the divine call on the seventh day recapitulates the creation week, suggesting that what is about to be revealed -- the Tabernacle and its liturgy -- is a new creation, a microcosm of the ordered universe. Moses enters the cloud (v. 18) as one enters a temple: the mountain has become the sanctuary, the cloud is the veil, and Moses is the high priest who alone passes through the veil into the presence of God. The forty days and forty nights that follow are a period of sustained divine revelation during which the heavenly pattern (tavnit) of the Tabernacle will be shown to Moses (25:9, 40). The chapter thus ends with Moses enveloped in the glory of God, suspended between earth and heaven, receiving the blueprint for the earthly dwelling-place of the God whose heavenly dwelling-place the elders have just glimpsed beneath the sapphire pavement.

---

*Note on the LXX (Vaticanus):* The Vaticanus text of Exodus 24 (pages 66-67) preserves the LXX version with several notable features. At v. 1, the LXX adds explicit identification of the speakers, rendering the Hebrew's implicit subject as kai Mōusei eipen ("and to Moses He said"). At v. 5, the LXX identifies the young men's offerings more specifically, and the sacrificial terminology (thusian sōtēriou, "sacrifice of salvation/peace") reflects the LXX's standard rendering of shelamim. At v. 7, the celebrated na'aseh venishma is rendered panta hosa elalēsen kurios poiēsomen kai akousometha ("all things whatsoever the Lord has spoken we will do and we will hear"), faithfully preserving the theologically crucial Hebrew word order. At v. 8, dam habberit becomes to haima tēs diathēkēs ("the blood of the covenant/testament"), the exact phrase quoted in the New Testament institution narratives. At v. 10, the LXX renders the difficult livnat hassappir as plinthon sappheirou ("a brick of sapphire"), taking livnah as "brick" (plinthos) -- a reading with potential theological resonance connecting the bricks of Egyptian slavery to the pavement of divine glory. The phrase ke'etsem hashamayim latohar is rendered hōsper eidos stereōmatos tou ouranou tē kathariotēti ("like the appearance of the firmament of heaven in purity"), introducing stereōma ("firmament") from the creation narrative. At v. 10, the Vaticanus reads ton topon hou histēkei ho theos tou Israēl ("the place where the God of Israel stood"), adding the spatial element of "standing" not explicitly present in the MT. At v. 11, the critical phrase lo shalach yado ("He did not stretch out His hand") is rendered ou diephōnēsen ("there was no disagreement/discordance") in some LXX traditions, though Vaticanus preserves a reading closer to the MT. At v. 12, the MT's luchot ha'even ("tablets of stone") is rendered tas plakas tas lithinas ("the stone tablets"), and the LXX adds kai tas entolas ("and the commandments") as a clarifying doublet. At v. 16, kevod YHWH is rendered hē doxa kuriou ("the glory of the Lord"), with the characteristic Septuagintal kurios for YHWH. At v. 17, the LXX renders esh okhelet as pur phlegon ("blazing fire"), replacing the Hebrew's "eating/devouring" metaphor with a more conventional description of fire. Throughout these pages, the Vaticanus text transitions from chapter 23 into chapter 24 in the lower portion of page 66, with chapter 24 continuing through page 67. The uncial script is clearly legible across all three columns, with occasional marginal notations visible.

--- LOGOS
